coredata.dc:title: “Green synthesis and performance evaluation of MnCuS-GQDs for energy storage systems: A comparative study”
coredata.dc:creator: Rafique, Hira
Iqbal, Muhammad Waqas
Hassan, Haseebul
Alharthi, Sarah
Amin, Mohammed A.
Afzal, Amir Muhammad
Ansari, Mohd Zahid
coredata.dc:description: Supercapacitors (SCs) are electrochemical energy-converting and storing devices that show great promise. Transition metal sulfides have received substantial research attention as an electrode material for SC applications. The pristine MnCuS demonstrated a poor storage capacity of 370C/g. MnCuS was doped with Graphene quantum Dots (GQDs) at various weight ratios to increase its performance. MnCuS-GQDs were evaluated in a three-electrode assembly, demonstrating an enhanced specific capacity of 883C/g. MnCuS-GQDs were also used to make a supercapattery (MnCuS-GQDs//AC). The MnCuS-GQDs//AC device performed exceptionally well electrochemically, having a 126C/g capacity. The energy of the MnCuS-GQDs reached 42.09 W.h/kg while retaining 1362 W/kg power. The MnCuS-GQDs//AC device was tested for 5000 cycles to determine its longevity. The findings revealed an amazing 89 % capacity retention. According to the findings, MnCuS-GQDs with a weight ratio of 95/5 % is a possible electrode materials for supercapacity applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nickel chloride
Manganese chloride
Copper chloride
Graphene quantum dots
Asymmetric supercapacitors
Supercapattery

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The researchers would also like to acknowledge the Deanship of Scientific Research, Taif University for funding this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Hydrothermal illusion for the synthesis of MnCuS. (b) Systematic approach to synthesize GQDs.
(a) XRD pattern for MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs. (b) SEM image for MnCuS-GQDs (c) FTIR spectrum for MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs (d) UV–Vis spectrum for MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs.
(a–c) XPS spectrum for Mn 2p, Cu 2p, and S 2p, respectively.
(a,b) BET isotherms for MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs.
(a–d) CV curves for MnCuS, MnCuS-GQDS3%, MnCuS-GQDs5%, and MnCuS-GQDs10%, respectively.
(a–d) GCD curves for MnCuS, MnCuS-GQDS3%, MnCuS-GQDs5%, and MnCuS-GQDs10%, respectively.
(a–d) Representation of specific capacity for MnCuS, MnCuS-GQDS3%, MnCuS-GQDs5%, and MnCuS-GQDs10% through CV and GCD readings. (e) EIS spectrum for MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs
(a) Systematic representation of MnCuS-GQDs//AC supercapattery. (b) CV curves for AC and MnCuS-GQDs in three-cell design. (c,d) CV and GCD curves for MnCuS-GQDs//AC supercapattery.

(a) Specific capacity for MnCuS-GQDs//AC through GCD. (b) b-fitting for MnCuS-GQDs//AC. (c) Stability measurements for MnCuS-GQDs//AC after 5000 cycles. (d) Ed and Pd for MnCuS-GQDs//AC. (e) EIS spectrum for MnCuS-GQDs//AC before and after 5000 cycles.
(a–d) Dunn's model applied on MnCuS-GQDs//AC device at 3 and 100 mV/s to compute capacitive and diffusive part.
Comparison of capacity retention and columbic efficiency for MnCuS-GQDs//AC device with literature.
Comparison of Ed and Pd for MnCuS-GQDs//AC device with literature.

head.ce:title: “Green synthesis and performance evaluation of MnCuS-GQDs for energy storage systems: A comparative study”

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Riphah International University, Campus Lahore, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Riphah International University, Campus Lahore, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Taif University, P.O. Box 11099, Taif 21944, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Taif University, P.O. Box 11099, Taif 21944, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yeungnam University, 280 Daehak-Ro, Gyeongsan, Gyeongbuk 38541, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yeungnam University, 280 Daehak-Ro, Gyeongsan, Gyeongbuk 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercapacitors (SCs) are electrochemical energy-converting and storing devices that show great promise. Transition metal sulfides have received substantial research attention as an electrode material for SC applications. The pristine MnCuS demonstrated a poor storage capacity of 370C/g. MnCuS was doped with Graphene quantum Dots (GQDs) at various weight ratios to increase its performance. MnCuS-GQDs were evaluated in a three-electrode assembly, demonstrating an enhanced specific capacity of 883C/g. MnCuS-GQDs were also used to make a supercapattery (MnCuS-GQDs//AC). The MnCuS-GQDs//AC device performed exceptionally well electrochemically, having a 126C/g capacity. The energy of the MnCuS-GQDs reached 42.09 W.h/kg while retaining 1362 W/kg power. The MnCuS-GQDs//AC device was tested for 5000 cycles to determine its longevity. The findings revealed an amazing 89 % capacity retention. According to the findings, MnCuS-GQDs with a weight ratio of 95/5 % is a possible electrode materials for supercapacity applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The need for energy is continually growing as our contemporary civilization develops. However, concerns about energy and the environment are also rising, posing significant hurdles to ensuring sustainable growth [1,2]. Because of the shortage of fossil fuels and rising energy demand, it is vital to investigate alternative energy sources and energy storage systems (ESS). The most frequently used ESS included batteries (LIBs) and supercapacitors (SCs) [3,4]. After years of extensive study, carbonaceous materials have emerged as very useful materials for ESS [5]. SCs have greater energy storage capacities and emerged as a viable energy storage technology. These characteristics make them suitable for electric cars. As research in this area continues, advancements are being made to improve the performance and efficiency of SCs, making them even more viable as a sustainable energy solution for the future [6,7]. SCs can charge and discharge faster than rechargeable batteries and store energy through redox reactions or double-layer effects. Nanomaterials, which have demonstrated comparatively high energy density [8,9]. Therefore substitute electrode materials that are low-cost, environmentally friendly, and exhibit good capacitive performance are required. MnS is a possible contender for high-performance SC applications because of its high conductance, theoretical specific capacitance, and great cyclic durability [10,11].

Furthermore, the environmental friendliness and low production cost of MnS make it a suitable alternative to the pricey and poisonous electrode materials utilized in typical SCs [12]. CuS is an excellent candidate for SC applications due to environmental friendliness. CuS is an especially enticing alternative to other electrode materials often used in traditional SCs [13].

Zhu et al. [14] developed CuCo2S4 nanoparticles using a solvothermal technique, which exhibited excellent cycle stability and 755 F/g. Although GQDs have received attention for their potential applications in energy-related fields, more research is required to comprehend their capability as electrode materials for electrochemical ESS fully. Small GQDs perform better than graphene sheets in terms of ionic charge adsorption due to their higher number of edge sites, which is essential for supercapacitive applications [15].

In this paper, MnCuS nanoparticle binary architectures were synthesized through a hydrothermal approach. Different characterization techniques were used to identify the synthesized materials. The electrochemical performance of MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs was tested using the electrochemical workstation. The GQDs/MnCuS with activated carbon were joined in a two-cell assembly to design supercapattery. Different key parameters such as specific capacity, energy, and power density were evaluated for the GQDs/MnCuS//AC device. The stability of the GQDs/MnCuS//AC device was tested.

Various chemicals were used including manganese chloride (MnCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), sodium sulfide hydrate (Na2S·9H2O), Acetylene black (AC), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), carbon black (CB), N-Methylpyrrolidone C5H9NO, GO sheets, H2SO4, and HNO3, all of which were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. In addition, deionized water (DIW) was utilized for solution preparation, sample washing, and electrolyte preparation. Foamed nickel and platinum (Pt) wire reference electrodes were obtained from Japan.

The first step in this study involved the preparation of graphene oxide (GO) sheets using the modified Hummers technique. Natural blackish graphite powder was used as the raw material. Next, the GO sheets were thermally deoxidized at 250–350 °C for 2 h, forming graphene sheets (GSs). H2SO4 and HNO3 were used to oxidize the GSs in a moderate ultra-sonication concentrated for 10–20 h. The presence of acids was eliminated by diluting in 260 ml of DIW and the resulting solution was passed through a 0.25-mm microporous membrane. The pH of the filtrate was adjusted to 8 using NaOH. The produced GSs (0.8 g) were dissolved into 50 ml DIW and placed in a 100
ml autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. The suspension was heated for 12 h at 200 °C to obtain a dark blackish suspension.

The MnCuS compound was synthesized using a traditional hydrothermal technique and a sintering process at a measured temperature. The specific preparation process involved adding 0.4 M of CuCl2, 0.4 M of MnCl2, and 0.8 M of sodium sulfide hydrate Na2S·9H2O into separate 30 ml beakers and stirring until a homogeneous solution was obtained. The solutions of MnCl2 and CuCl2 were then combined into a 150 ml beaker, resulting in a light-bluish liquid solution. Next, 30 ml of Na2S·9H2O sodium sulfide nano hydrate dissolved in deionized (DI) H2O was slowly added to the bluish solution, resulting in a color change from bluish to blackish due to the addition of sodium sulfide hydrates.

The fully formed solution was then transferred to an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 7 h. The resulting solution was washed several times with ethanol and deionized water, then moved into a patty dish before being fully dehydrated at 50 °C for 8 h. Finally, the resultant crystals of MnCuS were obtained, which appeared as a black powder and were marked as MnCuS for characterization and testing purposes. In the end, GQDs were incorporated into MnCuS heterostructure in three different weight ratios 3, 5, and 10 %. The synthesized method is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The working electrode for the electrochemical analysis was prepared by coating the active material onto the nickel foam (NF). The platinum wire served as the counter electrode and Hg/HgO as the reference electrode. A 1 M KOH solution was maintained at room temperature throughout the electrochemical tests in a standard cell. A 10 % PVDF binder, 75 % MnCuS active material, and 15 % acetylene black were used to make a slurry. This slurry is continuously stirred for a couple of hours to produce a standardized solution that was simple to coat with nickel foam. The slurry was then applied onto the NF surface, with a total mass of around 6 mg. It has been necessary to ensure that MnCuS completely coated the NF on both sides to prevent the nickel foam impact on the CV. A three-electrode formation is used to study cyclic voltammetry versus Hg/HgO. The charge-discharge GCD capacities are made in a similar setting at various current density values that range from 1.0 to 2.0 A/g. Eqs. (1) and (2) was used to compute the capacity through CV and GCD graphs [16].
(1)
Q s
=
1 mv
∫
V i
V f
I
X
VdV
(2)
Q s
=
I
X
t
m
∫
V i
V f
I
X
VdV indicated the CV curves area, m represented the load of material on NF, and v indicated the scan. The time duration between charging and discharging is represented by “t,” and the current by “I.”

The crystallinity of the MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs was checked through the X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique. Fig. 2(a) displays the XRD outcomes for GQDs and MnCuS. A peak, at a 2 theta value of 25°, is observed which corresponds to the GQDs. This peak is generated by the crystal plan (002) [17]. The XRD analysis of MnCuS revealed diffraction peaks at various 2-theta angles, 27.48°, 29.61°, 30.2°, 32.11°, 33.33°, 40.5°, 46°, 48.30°, 52.93°, and 59.72°. These peaks are produced by the crystal planes (100), (101), (102), (111), (103), (200), (110), (120), (114), and (311), respectively. The presence of MnS can be observed from the red color planes that match with JCPDS- No: 06–0518 while the presence of CuS can be observed from a black color plane that matches with JCPDS-78-0876 [18,19]. The XRD pattern for MnCuS-GQDs is well matched with the above-mentioned peaks, determining the successful synthesis of materials. The size of MnCuS-GQDs crystallite was observed using Scherer's formula:
(3)
D =
kλ
β cos θ
The observed crystallite size for MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs were 59 and 68 nm, respectively.

The shape and structure of MnCuS-GQDs were studied with SEM (Fig. 2b). According to the results, the MnCuS-GQDs sample exhibited a unique structure with evenly dispersed MnCuS nanoparticles and GQDs. The GQDs were evenly distributed, indicating a robust interaction with MnCuS nanoparticles. Furthermore, SEM scans revealed that the average particle size of MnCuS nanoparticles was 50–80 nm, whereas GQDs were 2–6 nm. The SEM study revealed details about the shape and structure of the MnCuS-GQDs sample, which might help researchers better understand its features and prospective uses.

The functional groups in MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs were studied using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 2c). The FTIR spectrum revealed distinct peaks for various functional groups. The OH stretching vibration was recorded at 3342 cm−1 in MnCuS. Peaks at 2229 and 2139 cm−1 were likewise observed in the sulfonate group, whereas 1344 and 889 cm−1 were observed in the SO4 group. Furthermore, the CH stretching vibration was detected at 2750 cm−1, whereas the C-O-C stretching vibration was detected at 1250 cm−1. At 1115 cm−1, the C-OH group was identified [20]. The FTIR spectra of MnCuS-GQDs showed CH stretching vibration at 2800 to 3100 cm−1 and the structural vibration of aromatic rings at 1460 to 1650 cm−1 which was due to the presence of GQDs. The FTIR study revealed vital information on the chemical composition and functional groups in MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs, which is critical for understanding their characteristics and prospective applications [21].

The optical characteristics of MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs were investigated using ultraviolet-visible (UV) spectroscopy (Fig. 2d). The broad absorption spike at 465 nm revealed the existence of MnCuS [22,23]. The presence of GQDs in the heterostructure of MnCuS resulted in increased absorption. MnCuS-GQDs have an absorption spike at 327 nm, which reflects a redshift from the MnCuS material [24]. The redshift is induced by the mixing of GQDs with MnCuS nanoparticles, which affects the electrical constitution of the mixture. The UV analysis showed the optical properties of MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs, which is important for their potential applications in optoelectronics and photovoltaics.

The outcomes of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to analyze the chemical structure and oxidation states of MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 3 (a–c). Mn 2p, Cu 2p, and S 2p spectra indicated characteristic peaks for the relevant elements. Peaks with binding energies of 637.3 eV and 652.3 eV were discovered in the Mn 2p spectra, correlating to the Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 states, accordingly [25]. Peaks in the Cu 2p spectra were seen at binding energies of 934
eV and 953.5
eV , respectively, belonging to Cu
2
p
3 / 2
and Cu
2
p
1 / 2
states. Also two satellite peaks were identified at 938 and 958 eV for Cu 2p [26]. The S 2p spectra showed two peaks with binding energies of 161.6 eV and 163.3 eV, which corresponded to the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 states, respectively [27].

The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method was used to measure the specific surface area, pore size distribution, and pore volume of MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs (Fig. 4a and b). The BET measurements revealed type IV isotherms for both materials. The hysteresis loops were of type H2, indicating that the pores are slit-like., the BET isotherm for MnCuS-GQDs had a steeper slope compared to MnCuS at low relative pressures, indicating a larger surface area for MnCuS-GQDs. According to the BET graph, the surface area of MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs is 37.33 and 43.21 m2/g. GQDs, which enhance the surface area of the composite material, account for the greater surface area of MnCuS-GQDs. MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs have pore volumes of 0.043 and 0.055 cm3/g, respectively. MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs have a mesoporous pore size distribution, with an average pore size of roughly 3–4 nm. The BET studies revealed crucial information on the surface area, pore size, and pore volume of MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs, which is relevant for their prospective uses in energy storage devices.

The electrochemical characteristics of MnCuS and its composites with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and CV analysis were used to investigate a solution within an operating potential (OP) of 0–0.6 V. Fig. 5 (a–d) represented the CV graphs for MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs composites. The quasi-rectangular form of the curves suggested the non-rectangular or faradaic behavior of MnCuS. The existence of redox peaks inside the material proved its battery-like behavior even further. MnCuS was physically combined with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in various weight ratios to optimize its performance. Adding GQDs to MnCuS improved the specific capacity and specific capacitance. The addition of GQDs to the MnCuS composite resulted in a greater current response, indicating that the two materials had synergistic effects. This highlights the potential benefits of using GQDs in MnCuS binary composites to increase conductivity and surface area, which are important in electrochemical properties. Incorporating GQDs into MnCuS electrodes improved the electrochemical stability of the MnCuS-GQDs composite electrodes. This stability is strengthened further by redox potential throughout fast charge-discharge cycles, as seen by a rise in the region under the curve with an increasing scan rate [28,29].

To validate our assumption that the best composite is MnCuS-GQDs 5 % and to investigate further each material's electrochemical behavior, the galvanostatic charge-discharge curves were investigated at room temperature. The current densities for GCD values were carried out from 1.0 to 2.0 A/g and potentials were maintained from 0.0 to 0.6 V. Fig. 6 (a–d) depicts the GCD graphs for all samples. Both the single and composite material GCD curves demonstrated nonlinear performance, indicating battery-like behavior. Furthermore, the discharge curves exhibit plateaus at all current densities, indicating that the materials operate in a faradaic way. The specific capacity of MnCuS-GQDs composites was discovered to be dependent on current density. The specific capacity was higher at low current densities and reduced at increasing current densities. The MnCuS-GQDs 5 % composite had the highest specific capacity of all the current densities examined. The greater conductivity and surface area supplied by GQDs caused the faster mobility of charge transfer and more active sites for electrochemical processes, resulting in improved electrochemical performance of the MnCuS-GQDs 5 % composite.

The synergistic effects of MnCuS and GQDs facilitate charge transfer kinetics and provide active sites for electrochemical processes. The MnCuS-GQDs 5 % composite electrode displays the highest
Q s
among all the samples tested, with a value of 836C/g, while MnCuS has the lowest
Q s
at 353C/g (Fig. 7a and b). The
Q s
of MnCuS-GQDs 3 % and MnCuS-GQDs 10 % electrodes are correspondingly 427.9C/g and 513.4C/g. The specific capacity through GCD reading for MnCuS, MnCuS-GQDs 3 %, MnCuS-GQDs 5 %, and MnCuS-GQDs 10 % showed the specific capacity of 370, 513, 883, and 612C/g at
j s
of 1.0 A/g (Fig. 7c and d). The aggregation of GQDs in MnCuS-GQDs-10 % can cause a decrease in composite material stability as the clusters of GQDs may detach from the electrode surface during cycling, resulting in a loss of redox-active sites. The excessive amount of GQDs may have a detrimental effect on the performance of electrode material. Additionally, the specific capacity increases as the scan rate decreases, implying improved charge storage capacity at lower scan rates [30,31].

The EIS spectrum for MnCuS and MnCuS-GQDs-5 % was represented in Fig. 7(e). MnCuS-GQDs-5 % composite material exhibits a significantly reduced ESR value (1.08 U) compared to MnCuS (1.23 U), which can be attributed to the strong interaction between MnCuS and GQDs. This interaction increases the material's electrical conductivity, and the adjacent arrangement of the two elements further contributes to the low ESR value [32]. Moreover, the EIS data indicate that the charge transfer resistance (Rct) for MnCuS-GQDs-5 % is lower than that of other materials. This suggests improved electrochemical kinetics and faster charge transfer at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The diameter of the semicircle on the real axis and Rct are directly proportional, and the NiMnCuS-GQDs-5 % composite material exhibits a small Rct among other materials. This is due to the fast electron transport between MnCuS and the highly conductive GQDs liquid. In actual device applications, GQDs were used as the cathode material.

Fig. 8(a) depicts a schematic of the device, comprising various supercapattery components. The MnCuS-GQDs and activated carbon (AC) were chosen as positive and negative electrode materials to construct a supercapattery. In the electrolyte of KOH, a porous membrane was used to separate the AC electrode from the battery-grade material. The AC electrode was given a negative potential to enhance the electrolyte ion absorption, while the MnCuS-GQDs electrode was given a positive potential, as it is a battery-grade material. MnCuS-GQDs electrodes typically have an OP from 0 − 0.6
V , whereas the potential window for capacitive electrodes is wider, usually ranging from −1 to 0 V. By comparing the CV curves of a MnCuS-GQDs electrode and AC, it can be observed that the AC is a capacitive electrode, while the MnCuS-GQDs is diffusive, owing to faradaic processes (Fig. 8b).

The MnCuS-GQDs//AC supercapattery CV curves were found to have a typical capacitive behavior at low potential and a redox potential at high potential (Fig. 8c). This behavior can be attributed to the electrode materials' capacitive and battery-like properties. At lower potentials, capacitive behavior takes precedence, resulting in a capacitive CV curve. The redox processes of MnCuS, a battery-grade material, begin to contribute at increasing potential, resulting in a redox peak in the CV curve [33,34]. This behavior is typical of faradaic processes, which include electron transport between the electrode and the electrolyte.

Interestingly, even at increased scan speeds, the form of the CV curve remained unchanged, which is a crucial feature of supercapattery electrodes. This behavior allows for efficient charge storage and discharge. The unmodified form of the CV curve reveals the excellent stability and longevity supercapattery electrodes, a vital need for long-term energy storage applications.

The MnCuS-GQDs//AC supercapattery GCD curves revealed a capacitive and diffusive involvement, typical of supercapattery electrodes (Fig. 8d). The triangular form of the GCD curves with a tiny hump suggests that the electrode materials contribute to capacitive and battery-like behaviors. The initial triangular form of the GCD curves indicates capacitive behavior, however, the slight hump on the discharge curve indicates the battery-like behavior.

The MnCuS-GQDs//AC supercapattery demonstrated a
Q s
of 126C/g at
j s
of 1.0 A/g (Fig. 9a). b-fitting was an efficient technique to compute the capacitive and diffusion-controlled charge storage mechanisms in a supercapattery. The appropriate b-value for a supercapattery should be 0.5–0.8 to achieve a balanced contribution from both processes [35]. For MnCuS-GQDs//AC supercapattery, the b-value was determined to be within this optimal region, demonstrating a combination of capacitive and diffusion-controlled charge storage behavior. The stability of the supercapattery device is critical in its practical use. In a stability test, the MnCuS-GQDs//AC supercapattery device was subjected to 5000 GCD cycles (Fig. 9c). After 5000 cycles, the device was very stable, with a capacity retention of 89 % and a coulombic efficiency of 94 %. The capacity retention indicates that the MnCuS-GQDs//AC device retained its energy storage capacity even after extensive cycling. The high coulombic efficiency of the MnCuS-GQDs//AC device indicates that it maintained its charge-discharge efficiency, ensuring consistent performance over time. Table 1 compares stability measurements for the MnCuS-GQDs//AC device with the literature. The energy density (Ed) and power density (Pd) of a supercapattery device are significant characteristics to consider when evaluating its performance. The Ed and Pd were calculated using the formulae below [36,37].
(4)
E =
Q × ∆ V
2 × 3.6
(5)
P =
E × 3600
∆ t d

Table 2 compares MnCuS-GQDs//AC
E D
and
P d
to earlier research. The
E D
of the MnCuS-GQDs//AC device was 42.06 Wh/kg, and the
P d
was 1362 W/kg (Fig. 9d). This shows that the MnCuS-GQDs//AC device has a high energy storage capacity while also being capable of swiftly delivering high power output. The impedance characteristics of the MnCuS-GQDs//AC device were examined using EIS before and after 5000 cycles (Fig. 9e). According to the EIS graphs, the device had an equivalent series resistance (ESR) value of 0.81 before cycling. This number increased to 0.88 after 5000 cycles. On the other hand, the change in ESR value was relatively low, indicating that the device remained stable through long durations of cycling. Dunn's model was also applied to CV curves of the MnCuS-GQDs//AC device:
(6)
i
v
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v 0.5
(7)
i
v
v 0.5
=
k 1
v 0.5
+
k 2

This model was utilized to investigate the capacitive and diffusive interactions of a MnCuS-GQDs//AC device at two different scan speeds, 3 and 100 mV/s (Fig. 10 ). The capacitive participation was 24.87 % at 3 mV/s, indicating that the device mainly relied on diffusive storage. At a greater scan rate of 100 mV/s, the capacitive participation increased drastically to 32.69 % due to the fast transferring of charges and ion diffusion at higher scan rates. The diffusive involvement was around 75 %, indicating that the device also utilized redox mechanisms to store energy. The unique combination of MnCuS-GQDs//AC with high energy and power density makes it an appealing alternative for energy storage systems.

This study describes the hydrothermal synthesis of novel MnCuS-GQDs electrode material for supercapattery devices. The composite material demonstrated a specific capacity of 883C/g at 1.0 A/g current density. The supercapattery also constructed using MnCuS-GQDs and AC showed exceptional electrochemical characteristics in a two-cell design. The supercapattery (MnCuS-GQDs//AC) exhibited a
Q s
of 126C/g and a
J s
of 1.0 A/g. The MnCuS-GQDs//AC device also provides 42.09 Wh/kg energy density while retaining 1362 W/kg power. The MnCuS-GQDs//AC device also demonstrated exceptional durability by maintaining 89 % of specified capacity even after 5000 charging and discharging cycles. This novel MnCuS-GQDs composite material can address the growing need for high energy and power density applications.

Hira Rafique and Muhammad Waqas Iqbal worked on data collection, analysis, and interpretation of results. Muhammad Waqas Iqbal and Haseeb ul Hassan and Mohd Zahid Ansari performed the calculation and write the manuscript. Sarah Alharthi, Mohammed A. Amin and Amir Muhammad Afzal helped during the calculation process and reviewed the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The researchers would also like to acknowledge the Deanship of Scientific Research, Taif University for funding this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2023.110297.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bundlelike CuCo2O4 microstructures assembled with ultrathin nanosheets as battery-type electrode materials for high-performance hybrid supercapacitors
ACS Appl. Energy Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Sun, X. Du, R. Wu, Y. Zhang, C. Xu, H. Chen, Bundlelike CuCo2O4 microstructures assembled with ultrathin nanosheets as battery-type electrode materials for high-performance hybrid supercapacitors. ACS Applied Energy Materials, 3 (2020) 8026-8037.
